narrative: As a white male applicant with a part-time work commitment, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, family background, and more to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 27 (well under the 36.70 average), low family income bracket, Year 1 school decile of 2, and Year 3 decile of 1—and predicted a 41% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your family income had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward success and passing the bar, likely because students from similar low-income backgrounds in the data often showed resilience and high motivation. Next in influence was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which significantly pulled the prediction toward failure, as this bottom-tier performance stood out sharply against the average decile of about 5.5 and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong grades. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, also nudging toward a passing prediction despite being low, reflecting that while not competitive, it didn't derail your chances as much as your later academic decile. Compared to typical students—who often enter with higher GPAs, LSATs around 37, full-time status (92% of the dataset), and mid-range deciles—your part-time plans and lower early rankings placed you in a tougher spot, amplifying the Year 3 decile's weight. It's understandable to feel discouraged, especially as a dedicated applicant balancing real-world commitments. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those school deciles, and exploring programs that value part-time students or offer support for those from low-income families. Building a narrative around your resilience could also help in reapplying or targeting schools with holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for future applications.
